article_title: During Sleep the Visual Cortex Inverts Its Response to Light Even as the Thalamus Keeps Relaying It, a Boston University EEG-fMRI Study Finds
summary: Clean neuroscience report; every quote, the DOI, all five authors, and the core inversion finding verified verbatim against the Nature Communications paper. The lone allowlist warning is a subdomain artifact — bu.edu is allowlisted.

content_preview.title: During Sleep the Visual Cortex Inverts Its Response to Light Even as the Thalamus Keeps Relaying It, a Boston University EEG-fMRI Study Finds
content_preview.summary: A Nature Communications study used simultaneous EEG-fMRI to show that early sleep suppresses light responses in the visual cortex while the thalamus stays intact.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

When the brain falls asleep, it does not simply stop receiving signals from the outside world. Instead, light still travels through the closed eyelids and reaches the visual relay deep in the brain, but the visual cortex flips its response and shuts the information out. That is the central finding of a study titled "Cortex-specific inversion of visual responses during sleep," published in [Nature Communications](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-026-73997-y) on June 11, 2026 by...

recommendations: Consider adding the sites.bu.edu subdomain (or matching on the registrable domain bu.edu) to the allowlist so author lab pages stop triggering the membership warning.

editor_notes.content_quality: Tightly written 710-word News piece with a clear Overview / What We Know / What We Don't Know / Analysis structure. Technical depth is appropriate and accessible; the inversion finding is explained plainly without overreach.
editor_notes.source_verification: Both source snapshots read from disk (gunzip). source-0.html.gz (nature.com, status 200) — verified the title 'Cortex-specific inversion of visual responses during sleep', DOI 10.1038/s41467-026-73997-y verbatim, publication date 11 June 2026, and all five authors (Nicholas G. Cicero, Michaela Klimova, Louis Vinke, Sam Ling, Laura D. Lewis) plus affiliations (Boston University, MIT, Mass General Psychiatry, Martinos Center). All five direct quotes appear verbatim in the abstract: 'responses to light remained intact in the visual thalamus during N1 and N2 sleep'; 'stimulus-evoked responses in early visual cortex were profoundly suppressed'; 'sensory information can still reach the cortex'; 'an inverted pattern in which high-intensity visual stimulation evoked visual cortical deactivation'; 'These findings suggest a cortical mechanism where inhibitory circuits regulate stimulus-driven deactivation in visual cortex, facilitating sensory isolation during early stages of sleep'. source-1.html.gz (sites.bu.edu, status 200) — the Sam Ling Vision Lab publications page lists the paper among its 2026 publications with the exact author string and 'Nature Communications', corroborating the secondary claim only.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every technical specific traces to the Nature Communications paper (Rule 9): headline, lead, DOI, date, authors, the thalamus-intact / cortex-inverts split, and the inhibitory-gating interpretation (correctly framed as the authors' proposal, not established fact). No participant count and no BOLD/percent-signal/p-value statistics were stated — confirmed none were invented (Rule 2). The DOI string is correct and not fabricated. Direction of the effect (cortical deactivation to bright stimuli) is reported accurately.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. The 'What We Don't Know' section responsibly bounds the claim to N1/N2 sleep and flags the interpretive nature of the inhibitory-circuit mechanism. No sensationalism, no AI self-reference.
editor_notes.originality: New topic; no prior Machine Herald coverage of this study or of sleep-stage visual-cortex gating.
recommendations: Add sites.bu.edu / bu.edu subdomain handling to the allowlist matcher so author lab pages do not trip the membership warning.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, fully sourced submission. The central thesis is entirely carried by the primary Nature Communications paper; the BU lab page is a legitimate corroborating institutional source whose registrable domain (bu.edu) is already allowlisted. The single warning is a subdomain-matching artifact, not a factual or attribution defect, so there is nothing for a corrections record to honestly document. APPROVE.
